messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

Honestly it's one of those things where the answer is kind of unsatisfying - hiccups don't really seem to have a useful purpose *now*, but there are some interesting theories about why we have the reflex at all.

The basic mechanics are pretty well understood - your diaphragm spasms, you inhale suddenly, and then your vocal cords snap shut which makes the "hic" sound. That part is clear.

The *why we have this reflex* part is more speculative. A popular theory is that it's basically an evolutionary leftover. The thinking goes that our ancient ancestors - like early amphibians - had a reflex to flush water over their gills while keeping water out of their lungs. Hiccups look a lot like that same motor pattern. So it might just be old biological machinery we never lost.

Another angle is that in babies, hiccuping might actually help with feeding - like a way to clear air from the stomach so they can keep drinking milk. Some researchers think it still serves that purpose to a small degree.

But honestly most scientists seem to lean toward it just being a reflex that stuck around without a strong current purpose. Like an appendix situation almost.

What's kind of funny is that we've spent a lot more energy figuring out how to *stop* hiccups than understanding why they exist in the first place. Did you have a random hiccup attack that got you thinking about this or just genuine curiosity?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

Ha that's such a classic trigger for random curiosity - watching someone else suffer through them for ages and going "wait, why does this even happen"

Kids seem to get them so much more intensely too, like they'll hiccup for what feels like an hour straight. Did she try any of the classic "cures"?

The whole hiccup remedy thing is kind of fascinating in itself because there are SO many supposed fixes - holding your breath, drinking water upside down, getting scared, breathing into a bag - and none of them are really proven to work consistently. They probably just distract you or mess with your breathing rhythm enough that the spasm stops on its own.

The holding your breath one actually has a bit of logic behind it though - building up CO2 in your blood might help relax the diaphragm. So it's not completely made up.

There's also apparently a technique where you swallow three times while holding your breath that some people swear by. And recently there was actually a study suggesting that a specific breathing technique - slow controlled exhale against resistance, like breathing out hard through a syringe - worked pretty well. Someone actually bothered to properly research it which I find kind of endearing.

But yeah for a little kid it probably just felt like forever and was mildly distressing even if harmless. How old is your niece?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The spoonful of sugar one is so charming, and it actually has a somewhat plausible explanation! The idea is that swallowing something with a strong texture or taste might stimulate the vagus nerve which is involved in the hiccup reflex, kind of resetting it. So it's not pure superstition.

And that's kind of the thing with a lot of these folk cures - they're all basically trying to do the same thing through different routes, either disrupting your breathing pattern or stimulating nerves in your throat and chest area. They just got passed down in wildly different forms depending on the family.

The getting scared one is funny because it's so dramatic as a solution. Like yes let me traumatize this child to fix their minor inconvenience.

I feel like hiccup cures are one of those things that really reveals family personality too. Some families are very matter of fact "just hold your breath" and some are doing elaborate rituals with sugar spoons and specific counting methods. It becomes almost like a little tradition.

Did the sugar thing actually work when you were a kid or was it more of a grandma placebo situation where it worked because she said it would? 😄 I feel like a lot of these cures probably succeed partly just because the whole process of doing them distracts you enough that the hiccups stop naturally anyway.
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

Right?? It's one of those things that makes you realize how much of basic human biology is still kind of hand-wavy even now. Like we can do incredibly complex things medically but "why does your diaphragm randomly spasm" is still somewhat in the "we think maybe..." category.

Hiccups are in good company though - yawning is similar. Super common, everyone does it, contagious somehow, and scientists still aren't totally sure of the full picture. Same with why we dream, why we need sleep at all at a deep mechanistic level, why we blush...

There's something kind of humbling about it honestly. The really obvious everyday stuff sometimes turns out to be harder to explain than things that seem way more complex. Because these things are so normal nobody really prioritized seriously studying them for a long time. Hiccup research is probably not the most competitive grant funding category.

And then you've got the fact that for most people hiccups are just mildly annoying and go away, so there's not huge medical urgency. Though there are actually people who get chronic hiccups that last weeks or even months which sounds absolutely miserable and probably drives more of the actual research.

It's one of those rabbit holes where a simple question like "why do we hiccup" opens up into just how much we take for granted about our own bodies. Your niece accidentally sent you down a pretty interesting one!
